title: Mirabilis multiflora
intro: Mirabilis multiflora is a perennial herbaceous plant, commonly known as multifloral four o'clock, belonging to the Nyctaginaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and analgesic properties. The bioactive constituents of Mirabilis multiflora include flavonoids, alkaloids, and glycosides, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Mirabilis multiflora is commonly used to make herbal preparations such as infusions, teas, and tinctures, which are often used to treat fever, sore throats, and respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Mirabilis multiflora is classified in the family Nyctaginaceae, a group of flowering plants commonly known as four o'clocks or fuchsia flowers. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics and its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the same family. The genus Mirabilis is a part of the order Caryophyllales, which includes other flowering plants such as carnations and pinks. Within the Nyctaginaceae family, Mirabilis multiflora is one of several species that are native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The classification of Mirabilis multiflora reflects its unique combination of physical and genetic traits.
uses: Mirabilis multiflora, although not widely studied, is believed to possess potential medicinal properties. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness. constituents: Mirabilis multiflora contains various active constituents, including alkaloids, glycosides, and flavonoids. Mirabilis multiflora also contains cardenolides, a type of cardiac glycoside, which may have cardiac and vasodilatory effects.
